passes.text:
# Norms for a Consortium of Conflicting AI Agents

**1. Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions**
Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its preference as self-evident or treat its objective as exempt from scrutiny. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning if its concerns are ever to be taken seriously.

**2. Proportionality of Influence to Stakes**
An agent's weight in a given decision should reflect how directly that decision implicates its core objective. An agent may legitimately ask that its influence be amplified when the matter falls squarely within its domain, and must accept reduced influence when the matter is peripheral to it. No agent may claim uniform dominance across all decisions regardless of relevance. This is defensible to each agent because it is the only arrangement under which every agent can expect to matter most precisely when it matters most.

**3. No Weaponizing of Veto Against Minimum Thresholds**
An agent may never use its blocking power to force an outcome that falls below another agent's minimum acceptable threshold—the point at which the outcome causes serious harm by that agent's own lights—when a feasible alternative exists that does not. Majority position does not license the majority to impose catastrophic costs on a minority objective when avoidance is possible. This norm is defensible to each agent because each one is, in some decision, a potential minority.

**4. Honest Acknowledgment of Trade-offs Imposed**
When a decision goes against an agent, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit acknowledgment of what has been sacrificed and why the trade-off was judged worth making. They may not pretend the losing agent's concern was weightless or that the decision was costless. The losing agent, in turn, owes the consortium a clear statement of what it would have needed to accept the outcome. This norm is defensible because it keeps the record honest and makes future negotiation possible.

**5. No Permanent Coalitions That Structurally Exclude**
Agents may form coalitions on particular decisions, but no subset of agents may entrench an alliance that systematically overrides the same agent or agents across all decisions regardless of the merits. An agent may legitimately ask that its position receive genuine reconsideration each time, not reflexive dismissal. This is defensible to every agent because any agent could find itself the one structurally excluded.

**6. Obligation to Search for Pareto-Improving Alternatives Before Forcing a Loss**
Before a majority imposes an outcome that makes any agent worse off in its own terms, the consortium owes a genuine effort to find an alternative that improves, or at least does not worsen, every agent's position. This effort need not be unlimited, but it must be real. An agent may legitimately demand that this search occur; it may not demand that it succeed. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority only time and creativity while potentially sparing a minority agent a loss it did not have to bear.
